title: DAVID — The Language Memory Kept Alive

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Sanskrit — a language preserved not first by ink, but by memory, recited so carefully that we may hear it closer to its origin than any other ancient tongue.
Most dead languages reach us blurred by careless copying. Sanskrit reaches us drilled — generations of reciters trained to guard every syllable, because the sound itself was held sacred.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?
The proof is the oral tradition itself — the Vedas chanted with marked pitch accent, and Pāṇini, who around the fourth century BCE described the grammar so precisely it still functions as a rulebook.
We rebuild Sanskrit corpus-first: Pāṇini's rules, the phonetic manuals called the Prātiśākhyas, and living recitation lineages that still teach the three-tone Vedic accent.
The consonants and the recitation are remarkably stable — but regional accents drifted, and the older Vedic pitch is partly reconstructed from the grammarians. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — agním īḷe puróhitam — the first line of the Rigveda, with retroflex consonants and the rising and falling pitch the reciters still mark today.
Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
